chapters.description: Using examples from parenting and romantic longing, he acknowledges that some attachment is irreducible and asks whether we can consent to its dukkha without utopian expectations.

chapters.title: Love without control
chapters.description: He turns toward a love that seeks no control, with metta arising through recognition of goodness, honesty, and moral beauty.

chapters.title: Abiding in goodness
chapters.description: The talk closes by linking loving-kindness to inner uplift, rest, and a heart that becomes less and less willing to live inside hatred and separation.

title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (2 of 5) Metta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores metta as something distinct from liking or wanting: not another form of preference, but a different stance of the heart. He traces how fear and craving contract the mind, then shows how loving-kindness can become an approach to pain, a purification practice, and a way of abiding with less control and more openness. The talk closes by linking metta to moral elevation, the recognition of goodness, and the possibility of resting in a warm, spacious heart.
short_summary: A clear reflection on metta as a non-possessive love that softens fear, approaches pain, and rests in goodness.

chapters.title: Love beyond liking
chapters.description: He opens by separating metta from ordinary liking and desire, arguing that love is not just another point on a spectrum of preference.

chapters.title: Wanting and fear
chapters.description: He connects wanting and fear as forms of clinging that contract the mind and turn experience into a transaction.

chapters.title: Metta as approach
chapters.description: Loving-kindness is presented as a move toward what is avoided, easing anxiety and helping pain loosen its grip.

chapters.title: What love is not
chapters.description: He clarifies that metta is not niceness, but an open and receptive love that lets go of control.

chapters.title: Attachment and family
chapters.description: Using a mentor’s distinction and a few literary references, he acknowledges that some love remains bound up with attachment and hurt.

chapters.title: Goodness as source
chapters.description: He turns toward a love that seeks no control and arises from seeing goodness and honesty clearly.

chapters.title: Moral elevation
chapters.description: He brings in the psychology of moral elevation to describe how witnessing goodness can move us into greater devotion to it.

chapters.title: Abiding in love
chapters.description: The talk closes by describing metta as an abode of rest and warmth that makes hatred less and less tenable.

title: Guided Meditation: Putting Down the Project of Your Life
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a gentle settling into posture, breath, and a quieter relationship to vigilance. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to the sense that worry, defensiveness, and the “project called my life” can be set down for a moment, making room for tranquility and not knowing. The practice gradually opens into the felt relief of letting go and the capacities that arise with a calmer nervous system: love, kindness, compassion, forgiveness, care, and peace.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, softening vigilance, and briefly setting down the burden of life as a project.

chapters.title: Arriving and settling
chapters.description: After the opening welcome, the meditation begins with settling the body and allowing time before trying to assume any inner posture.

chapters.title: Relaxed, alert posture
chapters.description: He orients the body toward a balance of openness and alertness, encouraging patience with the settling process.

chapters.title: Patience with worry
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward the mind's wayward longings and the habit of worrying before trust has fully arrived.

chapters.title: Breath over vigilance
chapters.description: Breath is used as a way to signal safety to the body and loosen defensiveness, with worry contrasted against tranquility.

chapters.title: Tranquility and not knowing
chapters.description: He emphasizes surrendering to breathing, silence, and uncertainty, letting the mind rest outside hyper-arousal.

chapters.title: Putting down the project
chapters.description: A central teaching frames the project of one's life as a burden and invites a temporary release of that self-management.

chapters.title: Returning without carrying
chapters.description: The meditation stays with the simplicity of the moment, acknowledging that the project will be picked up again without needing to be held constantly.

chapters.title: Wholesome qualities emerge
chapters.description: In the quiet that follows, he links settling with the Dharma and names the relief of letting go alongside love, kindness, compassion, forgiveness, care, and peace.

title: Dharmette: The Divine Abodes (1 of 5) Orienting to Love
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet, practical reflection on the Divine Abodes as a way of orienting the heart toward love rather than hate. He begins with the felt difference love makes when life is imperfect, then moves through non-hatred, ethical conduct, and the four Brahma-viharas as trainable resting places for the mind. The talk closes by inviting listeners to notice where aversion appears and to meet it with flexibility, improvisation, and a steadier return to love.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of the Brahma-viharas as a practical home base for the mind, especially when aversion is available.

chapters.title: Choosing Love First
chapters.description: Matthew frames love as a commitment that changes how experience lands, especially when life does not go well.

chapters.title: Why Hatred Arises
chapters.description: He reflects on samsara, the inevitability of things to object to, and how pain often seeks a target.

chapters.title: Non-hatred and Letting Go
chapters.description: The talk turns to non-hatred, renunciation, and the way quiet attention reveals the burden that aversion places on the heart.

chapters.title: Four Species of Love
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Brahma-viharas and interprets metta, compassion, mudita, and equanimity in vivid, practical terms.

chapters.title: Where the Mind Rests
chapters.description: He emphasizes that these qualities are not ideals to admire but places where the mind can increasingly come home.

chapters.title: Training Good Habits
chapters.description: The teaching shifts to discipline and repetition, suggesting that practice makes love more available and hate more effortful.

chapters.title: Depth and Breadth
chapters.description: Matthew broadens the teaching to show love as something that can pervade the mind deeply and extend outward across situations.

chapters.title: Closing Invitation
chapters.description: He ends with an invitation to watch aversion and stay flexible, with the promise of continuing the theme in later talks.

title: Guided Meditation: It's all come to this
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation centered on gathering the goodness of practice and meeting the present moment without violence. The talk moves from recollecting years of practice and the pain and wisdom that have led here, into a gentler way of relating to breath, body, and mind. He then turns toward refuge, showing how to rest in what is already here rather than trying to outthink uncertainty.
short_summary: A guided meditation on recollecting practice, softening into the present, and finding a deeper refuge in ordinary experience.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: He welcomes listeners and invites them to settle, alert but comfortable, for meditation.

chapters.title: Recollecting Practice
chapters.description: The meditation begins by recalling moments of willingness, retreat, practice, and friendship with the Dharma.

chapters.title: All Come To This
chapters.description: He gathers the whole arc of practice and life—pain, greed, hate, wisdom, and love—into the present moment.

chapters.title: Gently Tending Life
chapters.description: A long stretch of repeated phrases emphasizes tenderness, acceptance, and non-violence toward experience.

chapters.title: Ordinary Strategies
chapters.description: He names the ways we try to manage uncertainty through pleasure, planning, and rehashing the past.

chapters.title: Deeper Refuge
chapters.description: The meditation shifts toward refuge, resting in breath, body, and the present life as it is.

chapters.title: Winds Of Change
chapters.description: He closes by inviting trust in impermanence, letting change move through without harm.

title: Dharmette: Believing that Special People Exist
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver begins with a line from Rachel Kushner and uses it to explore why people imagine that special people exist. From there, he turns toward idealization, childhood dependence, ambivalence, and the difference between revering goodness and deifying a person. The talk ends by linking this to sympathetic joy and to practicing for the right reasons rather than for applause.
short_summary: A reflection on idealization, childhood longing, and learning to honor goodness without turning it into specialness.

chapters.title: The question of specialness
chapters.description: Brensilver opens with Rachel Kushner’s line about charisma and uses it to ask why the mind wants to believe in special people.

chapters.title: Projection and childhood
chapters.description: He looks at the pleasures and costs of idealization, then connects the pull toward special people to early dependence on powerful caretakers.

chapters.title: Growing up unresolved
chapters.description: He notes that adulthood does not simply resolve childhood, and suggests that the work is to notice our movement toward pedestal-making without needing to disparage anyone.

chapters.title: Reverence without ownership
chapters.description: The talk shifts toward maturity, ambivalence, and a form of reverence aimed at goodness itself rather than at specialness in a person.

chapters.title: Sympathetic joy
chapters.description: Brensilver uses mudita as a way of recognizing goodness without comparison, possession, or self-doubt.

chapters.title: Practice without applause
chapters.description: He closes with Norman Fischer on practicing because it is right and necessary, and reframes applause as praise for life and goodness more than for any individual.

title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Dharma Soak into You
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a gentle, receptive relationship with practice rather than an effort to force it. Matthew Brensilver emphasizes settling into the body, meeting agitation with patience and quiet love, and tending tenderly to any sense of distance from the Dharma. The talk later turns to recollecting Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha as a way of re-entering sensitivity, urgency, and trust, before ending in a simple offering of the heart with no strings attached.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling into the body, softening resistance, and letting recollection and care bring the Dharma closer.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens by finding some sweetness in sangha, technology, and lineage, then asks for a gradual settling of attention.

chapters.title: Body and Earth
chapters.description: Attention moves into the body on the earth, with repeated emphasis on gravity as a kind of embrace.

chapters.title: Meeting Unsettledness
chapters.description: Agitation, tension, and numbness are met with patience, tolerance, awareness, and quiet love.

chapters.title: When Dharma Feels Distant
chapters.description: Matthew names the felt remoteness of practice and invites listeners not to force connection, but to tend the sense of separation gently.

chapters.title: Recalling the Triple Gem
chapters.description: Recollecting Buddha, Dharma, and Sangha is presented as a way to remember, soften, and enter greater sensitivity and care.

chapters.title: Courage To Keep Looking
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into the courage of meeting this moment as it is, staying open to surprise rather than assuming we already know it.

chapters.title: Offering Without Strings
chapters.description: The close releases expectation and simply offers the heart to the Dharma, without insisting on any result.

title: Dharmette: Fear and Love and Dana
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores fear as a useful survival system that can also overlearn danger and narrow the heart. Using the story of Joao, a man who became radically generous after a stroke, he asks how fear constrains love, generosity, and our sense of what matters. The talk closes by returning to Thich Nhat Hanh on meeting fear directly, suggesting that courage and honest awareness can soften worry and open a more radical love.
short_summary: A reflection on fear’s protective role and how less fear may open generosity, love, and courage.

chapters.title: Fear Keeps Us Safe
chapters.description: Matthew begins by describing fear as a powerful survival mechanism that helps animals like us detect danger and stay protected.

chapters.title: Fear Of Suffering
chapters.description: He turns to the difference between sensitivity to suffering and fear of suffering, especially fear of others’ pain.

chapters.title: Joao’s Sudden Generosity
chapters.description: A story about Joao, who becomes unusually generous after a stroke, serves as the central example for the talk.

chapters.title: Brains And Freedom
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the role of brain injury, survival design, and the limits fear places on spontaneous giving.

chapters.title: What Love Might Be
chapters.description: He asks what love would look like if it were less constrained by fear and by a world read mainly as threat.

chapters.title: Social Rules And Self-Consciousness
chapters.description: He considers how custom, entitlement, and self-consciousness can narrow generosity, while imagining small acts of freer giving.

chapters.title: Meeting Fear Directly
chapters.description: Matthew brings in Thich Nhat Hanh’s teaching that fear is eased by acknowledging it and looking into its source.

chapters.title: Courage And Radical Love
chapters.description: He ends with the idea that opening to finitude and mortality can melt worry and make love more radical.

title: Guided Meditation: Vitality and Release, In Breath, Out Breath
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on how the in-breath and out-breath can be felt as different but complementary ways of practicing: vitality on the in-breath, release on the out-breath. He encourages a steady, simple attention that learns to welcome both alertness and surrender, and then extends that felt sense into the claim that this moment is not a dress rehearsal but the place where life is actually lived. The talk stays close to breath, posture, and the emotional textures of grasping, fear, brightness, and letting go.
short_summary: A guided meditation on balancing enlivening in-breaths with releasing out-breaths, and recognizing this moment as the place where life is lived.

chapters.title: Arriving and orienting
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a warm welcome and a check-in with the heart, then names the basic theme of learning the rhythms of practice.

chapters.title: Vitality and surrender
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the idea that some practitioners lean toward the in-breath while others prefer the out-breath, framing maturity as learning to love both.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: He invites a comfortable posture and a bright, steady presence as the body breathes in and out.

chapters.title: The in-breath's vividness
chapters.description: Attention turns to the rush, brightness, and poignancy of inhalation, including the way it can disrupt monotony and remind us not to miss our lives.

chapters.title: Releasing the out-breath
chapters.description: The out-breath is explored as a letting go of tension, vigilance, fear, and subtle ownership, with repeated encouragement to breathe out.

chapters.title: Breath and clarity
chapters.description: The talk deepens into the breath cycle as a moment-by-moment expression of clarity, release, brightness, and darkness.

chapters.title: Letting go of becoming
chapters.description: Brensilver links the out-breath with softening the impulse to become someone slightly better, and the in-breath with shaking off the idea that this moment is only rehearsal.

chapters.title: This is our life
chapters.description: The closing section widens the frame: meditation is not separate from life, because life is found in breath, thought, feeling, seeing, and hearing here and now.

title: Dharmette: Aperture of Attention in Meditation and Life
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver uses the image of an aperture to explore how attention can narrow or widen in meditation and in daily life. He traces when a smaller field is stabilizing, when a wider one is called for by compassion or service, and why the skill lies less in ideology than in timing. The talk stays practical and reflective, with a quiet emphasis on noticing what is wise now rather than forcing one mode of practice.
short_summary: A practical talk on when to narrow or widen attention in meditation, suffering, compassion, and daily life.

chapters.title: Aperture of Attention
chapters.description: He introduces attention as a spotlight whose width can vary, using the breath and the six sense gates as examples.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: He explains why practice often begins with a narrower field, especially when experience feels too intense or uncertain.

chapters.title: Opening With Stability
chapters.description: He describes how attention can widen after some settling, and why practice is not a simple beginner-versus-advanced hierarchy.

chapters.title: Life Beyond Meditation
chapters.description: He extends the same pattern to daily life, asking what the right scope of concern is in a given moment or day.

chapters.title: Contraction and Compassion
chapters.description: He challenges the assumption that contraction is always bad and expansion always good, emphasizing skillfulness over moral ranking.

chapters.title: When Narrowing Helps
chapters.description: He gives examples of overwhelm, helplessness, and painful political absorption where narrowing attention can be protective and practical.

chapters.title: When Wide Attention Heals
chapters.description: He offers the counterexample that distress or depletion can sometimes call for a much wider field, including service and giving.

chapters.title: Wise Attention Now
chapters.description: He closes by distinguishing care from resignation and returning to the central question of what attention is appropriate now.

title: Guided Meditation: Vulnerability & Courage
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a quiet guided meditation on vulnerability as a source of steadiness rather than weakness. The practice moves from settling the body and refreshing curiosity, into reflections on being undefended, allowing experience to pass through without hardening. He closes by linking courage to a balanced middle way and to a heart that can remain open without losing its center.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting experience with vulnerability, openness, and courage.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: He invites listeners to get cozy, but not too cozy, and to bring relaxed, energetic openness to body and mind.

chapters.title: Fresh Curiosity
chapters.description: The practice turns toward sincerity as a willingness to be surprised, with attention resting evenly in the present moment.

chapters.title: Breathing and Waking
chapters.description: A brief settling period emphasizes breathing, relaxing, and waking up to experience as it is.

chapters.title: Vulnerability as Strength
chapters.description: He reframes tenderness and vulnerability as the basis of real power, not brittle self-protection.

chapters.title: Undefended Heart
chapters.description: The talk deepens into the idea of being undefended without losing center, allowing softness, clarity, and love to coexist.

chapters.title: Attuning to Courage
chapters.description: Listeners are guided to sense their own power and to meet the moment with an open chest and a readiness to let feeling move through.

chapters.title: Middle Way of Courage
chapters.description: He brings in Aristotle’s image of courage between recklessness and cowardice, using it to point toward balanced, grounded openness.

chapters.title: Nothing to Defend
chapters.description: The meditation closes by suggesting that when there is no territory to protect, defensiveness softens and confidence remains.

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Various) A few thoughts on Wednesday November 6th 2024.

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

title: Metta for Self
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a talk and guided practice on metta for oneself, beginning with a broad framing of love as something that makes human life bearable. He distinguishes the four Brahma-viharas as different species of love, then spends much of the talk showing how self-mettta can work as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting whatever pain arises. The practice settles into repeated phrases of blessing and a steady invitation to see oneself through kind, merciful eyes, ending with a sense of love as vast and measureless.
short_summary: A talk and guided practice on self-directed loving-kindness that treats metta as cultivation, concentration, and a way of meeting hidden pain.

chapters.title: Love and human life
chapters.description: He opens by saying he wants to talk about love, then frames love as what makes the human situation bearable.

chapters.title: Four kinds of love
chapters.description: He names the Brahma-viharas as four species of love and distinguishes metta, compassion, joy, and equanimity.

chapters.title: How metta works
chapters.description: He explains metta as cultivation and as a radical sensitization to goodness, especially when the practice becomes quiet and focused.

chapters.title: What metta reveals
chapters.description: He describes how loving-kindness can also pull grief, rage, numbness, or fear to the surface, and suggests meeting that material with kindness.

chapters.title: Finding your own path
chapters.description: He encourages each person to find a native, sustainable way into love, then begins turning toward direct self-directed practice.

chapters.title: A merciful gaze
chapters.description: He invites listeners to see themselves through kind eyes and imagine how a deeply loving person would look at them.

chapters.title: Blessing the self
chapters.description: He brings in the example of a teacher's compassionate gaze, then moves into repeated phrases of wishing safety, ease, and happiness for oneself.

chapters.title: Problems dissolving
chapters.description: He emphasizes that loving-kindness does not solve problems so much as soften them, and repeats that the burden of 'my life' can be put down.

chapters.title: What has not been loved
chapters.description: He returns to the parts of experience that have not yet been loved and closes with a note that love makes the mind vast and measureless.

title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (5 of 5): A Training in Loving Well
speaker: Matthew Brensilver

title: Dharmette: Interpersonal Dharma (4 of 5): Honoring the Neurosis/Pain of the Other
speaker: Matthew Brensilver

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

speaker: Matthew Brensilver

title: Moral Elevation, Moral Disgust and the Vastness Required to Open to Dukkha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can sensitize us to goodness without losing contact with suffering. He moves from moral elevation and the force of ordinary courage into moral disgust, asking how care can stay clear of hatred, cynicism, and political reactivity. The talk gradually widens into a reflection on vastness, mortality, and impermanence, ending with Ajahn Chah’s tea cup as a way to hold life with gratitude and less fear.
short_summary: A reflection on goodness, moral disgust, and the need to widen the mind enough to meet suffering without turning to hatred.

chapters.title: Opening the Heart
chapters.description: Matthew introduces kama muta and moral elevation as ways the heart is moved by goodness.

chapters.title: Wesley Autry's Courage
chapters.description: He tells the subway rescue story and uses it to show how ordinary bravery can evoke and transmit moral beauty.

chapters.title: Sensitivity to Goodness
chapters.description: The talk turns toward metta, Rumi, and the way devotion can make goodness feel ordinary, embodied, and shareable.

chapters.title: Moral Disgust
chapters.description: Matthew contrasts moral elevation with moral disgust and warns that ethical feeling can be pulled toward aversion and brutality.

chapters.title: Prophetic Speech
chapters.description: Using Frederick Douglass and Heschel, he considers moral language that burns past ordinary conscience without collapsing into moralizing.

chapters.title: Distance from Rumination
chapters.description: He brings in self-distancing research to distinguish helpful reflection from looping pain and to suggest a wider perspective on suffering.

chapters.title: Widening the Horizon
chapters.description: The talk expands into global care, mortality, and Kore-eda's After Life as a glimpse of being less trapped by becoming.

chapters.title: Already Broken
chapters.description: Matthew ties cessation of becoming to the broken tea cup teaching and shows how impermanence can open the heart to love and gratitude.

description: (Big Bear Retreat Center) These recordings are available publicly from a retreat held in person at Big Bear Retreat Center. Learn more about the center and upcoming offerings for retreats in nature, gathered in community. bigbearretreatcenter.org/upcoming-retreats/ Healing the Self, Loving the Self, Forgetting the Self Matthew Brensilver July 9 - 13, 2024 Co-sponsored by Insight Retreat Center (IRC) and Big Bear Retreat Center In this silent retreat, we explore how mindfulness supports the healing of the self. Many of the Buddhist teachings help us to tend to painful memory, old pains and the habits that compound suffering. As we become more gentle and loving towards experience, the self becomes less and less of a preoccupation. The more completely we accept ourselves, the easier it becomes to forget the self and rest in an awareness unencumbered by self-consciousness. This retreat includes sitting and walking meditation instructions, and dharma talks.

title: Dharmette: Reflections on words from Ajahn Sucitto
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a short teaching from Ajahn Sucitto and uses it to unfold a quiet map of the path: generosity, ethics, renunciation, discernment, persistence, and patience. He moves from the felt meaning of sharing to the practical question of how to direct energy, make choices, and time action well. The talk stays grounded in ordinary examples and in the sense that practice is less about intensity than about steady, responsive care.
short_summary: A reflection on Ajahn Sucitto’s teaching that traces generosity and the rest of the path as expressions of interdependence, ethical care, and patient discernment.

chapters.title: Reading the quote
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the Ajahn Sucitto passage and names the cluster of qualities it gathers together.

chapters.title: Generosity as interdependence
chapters.description: He expands generosity beyond material giving into an attitude shaped by relation, example, and shared blessing.

chapters.title: Sharing Dharma wisely
chapters.description: The talk turns to how advice lands, why example matters, and how generosity can be expressed without attachment to view.

chapters.title: Ethics and kindness
chapters.description: Matthew links interdependence with ethical choice, then pauses with a story and a study of random acts of kindness.

chapters.title: Morality and renunciation
chapters.description: He moves through self-respect, non-regret, and the question of enough, using personal anecdotes to show discernment in action.

chapters.title: Steady persistence
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts from peaks of effort to the daily steadiness that supports lasting change.

chapters.title: Patience and timing
chapters.description: Matthew closes with patience as sensitivity to ripeness, causality, and when a move is truly called for.

chapters.title: Closing reflections
chapters.description: He thanks Ajahn Sucitto, offers the teaching for consideration, and ends with a brief farewell.

title: Metta Practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Spirit Rock Meditation Center) Afternoon Sit with Instructions
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver presents metta as something more than a pleasant mood: a capacity that makes insight practice bearable, especially when the heart meets the full intensity of suffering. He moves through the four brahmaviharas as four distinct kinds of love, then explains how metta can cultivate warmth, steady the mind, or bring buried grief and fear to the surface for healing. The talk closes with a simple practice of repeating phrases, listening for their effect, and widening the field of care to others without denying war, oppression, or loss.
short_summary: A grounded teaching on metta as the love that steadies insight, softens suffering, and widens naturally into compassion for all beings.

chapters.title: Love and Insight
chapters.description: Metta is introduced as essential to opening to the human condition without overwhelm, not as an optional extra to insight practice.

chapters.title: Four Species of Love
chapters.description: The talk reframes the brahmaviharas as metta, compassion, sympathetic joy, and equanimity, each meeting a different condition of life.

chapters.title: Why Love Matters
chapters.description: Matthew explains why wisdom is incomplete without love and how metta can function as cultivation, concentration, or purification.

chapters.title: Meeting What Hurts
chapters.description: Metta is described as a practice that can draw grief, rage, and confusion into view and hold them with kindness rather than avoidance.

chapters.title: Settling the Body
chapters.description: The instruction turns practical, emphasizing a sustainable posture, relaxation, and a quiet refusal to perform practice for anyone.

chapters.title: Remembering Goodness
chapters.description: Listeners are guided to bring to mind an image of goodness, sense the heart center, and let warmth soften clinging and self-harshness.

chapters.title: Abiding in Kindness
chapters.description: The teaching deepens into a felt sense that kindness makes it safe to let go and reminds the mind that aversion and fear cannot truly protect it.

chapters.title: Extending the Wish
chapters.description: The phrases widen from oneself to others, turning personal longing into a more universal wish for happiness, safety, and peace.

chapters.title: A Sublime Abiding
chapters.description: The close acknowledges suffering in the world while affirming that this species of love is not diminished by it and can be a noble dwelling of mind.

title: Guided Meditation: Listening to the Mind of Buddha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that gradually shifts from settling into the room toward a practice of listening: first to breath, body, sound, and the darkness behind closed eyes, then to a deeper receptivity he links to Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines. The talk emphasizes trusting awareness, letting silence beckon, and resting without over-managing experience. It closes by naming practice as a way to stop endlessly coping and simply be awake.
short_summary: A guided meditation on listening inwardly, trusting awareness, and resting beyond the compulsion to manage experience.

chapters.title: Arriving and Settling
chapters.description: A brief welcome leads into the invitation to sit and settle into stillness.

chapters.title: Silence Beckons
chapters.description: He names the silence and begins orienting attention toward the meditative field.

chapters.title: Simple Objects of Attention
chapters.description: The instruction turns to breath, body, sound, darkness behind the eyes, and possibly phrase or mantra as supports for attention.

chapters.title: Listening to Cohen
chapters.description: Leonard Cohen’s hummingbird lines become a doorway into the teaching about listening beyond the speaker and toward what does not need to be forced.

chapters.title: Trusting Awareness
chapters.description: He frames practice as listening to the mind of a Buddha, emphasizing receptivity and trust in awareness rather than effortful control.

chapters.title: Forgiveness and Rest
chapters.description: The meditation deepens into forgiving the mind, resting in silence, and meeting sorrow with a more spacious stance.

chapters.title: Patience and Urgency
chapters.description: He explores the balance between spiritual urgency and abiding patience as practice matures.

chapters.title: Beyond Coping
chapters.description: The close points to resting from endless coping, releasing the habit of sorting everything out, and finding wakefulness in simple being.

title: Ill-Will & Metta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how aversion narrows experience, and how metta opens a more honest and compassionate way to meet suffering.

chapters.title: Opening the problem
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces ill-will and frames it as a familiar way of suffering that the talk will examine closely.

chapters.title: The Dharma as a broad remedy
chapters.description: He uses pharmacology to argue that Dharma works through many mechanisms, not just the surface sign of a calm mind.

chapters.title: Purification and practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes concentration from purification and says the path includes peaks, valleys, and whatever is present now.

chapters.title: What and how
chapters.description: The talk turns to the 'what' and the deeper 'how' of practice, showing how motivations shape what we seem to know.

chapters.title: Craving shapes the view
chapters.description: He explores how craving and aversion thicken experience, using his own childhood sensitivity to show how ill-will can become a strategy for managing pain.

chapters.title: Mara and the turning inward
chapters.description: Brensilver describes the habitual impulses to approach, avoid, or ignore, then shows how clinging and aversion reinforce helplessness and suffering.

chapters.title: The heat of aversion
chapters.description: Using the boiling-pot simile, he explains how ill-will obscures judgment, while Agnes Callard, Ruth King, and the Dalai Lama help refine the view of anger as partially functional but never transformative.

chapters.title: Facing and owning anger
chapters.description: He turns to noticing ill-will directly, describing radical accountability as a training that stops externalizing blame without denying external causes.

chapters.title: Metta as antidote
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to metta as warm friendliness, free of possessiveness and control, and as a way of being moved by goodness and finding a home in it.

title: Guided Meditation - Grief and Compassion (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on grief that treats sorrow as a natural part of loving in an impermanent world. The practice moves from settling the body into phrases of tenderness, mercy, and reverence, with brief references to Leonard Cohen, anicca, and Stephen Levine to steady the listener in uncertainty. The tone stays intimate and practical, emphasizing that compassion is not an extra but a necessity when loss keeps arriving.
short_summary: A guided meditation on grief, impermanence, and compassion that invites the heart to meet sorrow with tenderness and mercy.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins by arriving and settling the body into the practice.

chapters.title: Welcoming the Body
chapters.description: Attention opens to bodily experience as a place to receive the practice gently.

chapters.title: Love and Grief
chapters.description: Brensilver names grief as woven into loving in a changing world and frames compassion as a necessity.

chapters.title: Impermanence and Tenderness
chapters.description: He invokes anicca to explain why change is startling and encourages mercy and love toward the heart.

chapters.title: Traveling in Fog
chapters.description: A Stephen Levine image of grief as traveling at night in fog supports patience with limited clarity.

chapters.title: Holding Sorrow
chapters.description: The practice shifts into explicit wishing and a steadier acceptance of what is here.

chapters.title: Love Deepened
chapters.description: He reflects that love without grief can be naive, and that loss can enrich love.

chapters.title: Tender Phrases
chapters.description: The meditation closes with repeated phrases of tenderness and reverence for the grieving world, ending in a quiet close.

title: Wednesday Evening Reflection: Rohini's Enlightenment Poem
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on a line from Rohini’s enlightenment poem: the path is not a line on a map, but a great shining world, enter wherever you like. He uses that image to gently undo the pressure to fit Dharma practice into a fixed shape, suggesting instead that we discover our own belonging within it. The talk closes with an encouragement to affirm one’s efforts, rest well, and continue noticing how apparent walls may become doorways.
short_summary: A brief reflection on belonging in Dharma, centered on the image of the path as a great shining world.

chapters.title: A shining world
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the poem’s image of the path as a great shining world rather than a fixed route.

chapters.title: Not fitting in
chapters.description: He examines the tendency to feel we must mold ourselves to match Dharma or Dharma culture.

chapters.title: Enter anywhere
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward the freedom to begin practice from whatever conditions we actually have.

chapters.title: Belonging and home
chapters.description: He links belonging with a powerful human motivation and describes Dharma as something that can come to feel like home.

chapters.title: Nothing left behind
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that ultimately we are not forced to abandon any part of the heart in order to practice.

chapters.title: From every direction
chapters.description: He repeats the invitation to enter from anywhere, even when we feel awkward, remote, or contorted.

chapters.title: Affirming effort
chapters.description: The talk softens into encouragement to value one’s efforts without turning that recognition into self-absorption.

chapters.title: Rest and continue
chapters.description: He ends with a blessing to practice well, rest well, and keep going as new Dharma doors appear.

title: Death and the Poignancy of Life
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (New York Insight Meditation Center) William James said that death was the ‘worm at the core’ of the human condition that turns us all into ‘melancholy metaphysicians.’ A century later, awareness of mortality is documented to affect our thinking and emotional lives in powerful ways. It figures prominently in Buddhist practice. In what ways does consciousness of death distorts our view and lead us away from wisdom and compassion? Alternatively, how can we open to the truth of finitude such that our heart is softened? Can we intuit the freedom or love that might be released were we more deeply at peace with our mortality? In this evening program, we’ll consider the way death can harden or soften our heart – and how dharma practice might lead us to a life that feels complete. All are welcome.
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on death not as a grim abstraction but as a way to clarify what matters, soften the heart, and loosen the pressure to build a story around the self. He moves through examples from literature, Buddhist teachings, psychology, and personal memory to show how mortality can either harden us into fear or open us toward grief, love, and responsibility. The talk ends by returning to practice as a kind of training in letting go and in making friendship with the mind.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on mortality, grief, and how accepting finitude can open compassion and make life feel more complete.

chapters.title: Opening the topic
chapters.description: He names death as the evening’s theme and uses a satirical Onion story to point to the acquisitive, grasping quality that can enter meditation.

chapters.title: Writing and dancing
chapters.description: A passage from Nicole Krauss becomes a way to contrast self-building and present-moment immediacy, recasting practice as more like dancing than narrating a life story.

chapters.title: Birth and mortality
chapters.description: He widens the frame to the rarity of being born at all, then introduces the five remembrances and Ajahn Lee’s praise of aging, illness, and death as treasures.

chapters.title: Practicing with death
chapters.description: He describes year-to-live practice, the social sensitivity of talking about mortality, hospice work, and the way one’s death shapes the lives of others.

chapters.title: Resisting and meaning
chapters.description: Stephen Jay Gould and Irvin Yalom help him balance acceptance with the reality that death can feel like an enemy and that death anxiety drives much human life.

chapters.title: Finitude and grief
chapters.description: Drawing on a book about secular faith, he argues that finitude makes responsibility possible and that dharma practice requires the courage to grieve, both personally and collectively.

chapters.title: Loss makes life vivid
chapters.description: Catherine Schultz and Walt Whitman are used to show that impermanence sharpens attention, makes ordinary life precious, and asks us to keep watch rather than keep control.

chapters.title: Fear, identity, and softness
chapters.description: He turns to Freud, Terror Management Theory, and Pema Chödrön to show how mortality can trigger defensive identity, or, when groundlessness is met well, awaken kindness.

chapters.title: Becoming and letting go
chapters.description: A Buddhist teaching on craving for becoming leads into an account of practice as surrendering the future, loosening self-clinging, and rehearsing the deathless by relinquishing control.

title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto teaching about why stopping feels difficult: the self stays busy trying to find ground, while practice asks for a deeper loving acceptance of experience as it is. He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.
short_summary: A reflective talk on busyness, selfing, and how loving acceptance can become a real ground for practice.

chapters.title: Ajahn Sucitto's quote
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the teaching and begins unpacking the claim that stopping is hard because self is always busy searching for ground.

chapters.title: Busyness as human condition
chapters.description: He broadens the theme into ordinary life, where even quiet time and retreat can feel organized around avoiding stillness.

chapters.title: Solitude and avoidance
chapters.description: Pascal and David Foster Wallace help frame the fear of being alone with the deeper pain that silence can expose.

chapters.title: Self, agitation, and comparison
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the subtle connection between selfing, defensiveness, and the habit of evaluating oneself as an object.

chapters.title: Mind and narrative self
chapters.description: He uses Nisargadatta and Dennett to suggest that what feels like a solid self is more like a narrative center folded out of experience.

chapters.title: Loving the mess
chapters.description: A long example from Heather Havrilesky and references to teachers like Ajahn Chah and Ajahn Sumedho support a gentler, less combative way of meeting our own messiness.

chapters.title: Non-dwelling and mortality
chapters.description: The talk shifts toward not dwelling in self at all, then toward death, Parfit, and the relief that comes when the self is less of a prison.

chapters.title: Closing blessing
chapters.description: Matthew ends with repeated invocation, a blessing for welfare, and a brief closing exchange with the group.

title: Practice Note: Note your life away
speaker: Matthew Brensilver

title: guided meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (New York Insight Meditation Center) Meditation practice cultivates a diverse set of attentional, emotional and introspective skills. Central to the practice of mindfulness is the stabilization of attention. But before our attention stabilizes, practice can be overstimulating. This talk will explore the process through which the mind comes to rest. In developing this steadiness, equanimity (the capacity to fully permit the flow of both pleasure and pain) is a vital skill. We will see how concentration and equanimity reinforce each other and support a deeper understanding of ourselves. And how this stability, in turn, makes space for the heart to respond with joy and compassion.
